evidences.title: 2026 Makerfield by-election
evidences.content: [edit] Ashton-in-Makerfield library The Makerfield constituency contains the towns of Ashton-in-Makerfield and Hindley as well as other residential areas on the western outskirts of Greater Manchester. Considered part of the red wall "Red wall (British politics)") of working-class areas in northern and central England that traditionally vote Labour, Makerfield has been a safe seat for the Labour Party "Labour Party (UK)") since its creation in 1983 from parts of Ince "Ince (constituency)"), Wigan "Wigan (constituency)") and Leigh "Leigh (constituency)"). Makerfield was represented from 1987 to 2010 by one-time cabinet minister Sir Ian McCartney, then by Yvonne Fovargue until 2024. In 2024, Josh Simons held the seat with a 5,399-vote (13.4%-points) majority over Reform UK. evidences.title: 2026 Makerfield by-election - Wikipedia
evidences.content: On 14 May 2026, Simons resigned his seat in parliament in order to allow Andy Burnham to contest the by-election so as to be able to stand for the Labour leadership, in accordance with party rules that require any candidate for leader to be a member of the Parliamentary Labour Party. Simons's resignation was formalised on 18 May 2026, when he was appointed Crown Steward and Bailiff of the Chiltern Hundreds, and the writ of election was moved in the House of Commons on 19 May 2026. A notice of election was published on 20 May 2026, confirming 18 June 2026 as the date for the vote, with nominations closing on 26 May. [...] 9. ↑ McKenzie-Folan, Alison (20 May 2026). "Notice of Election – UK Parliamentary Election for the Borough Constituency of Makerfield" (PDF). Wigan Council. Archived (PDF) from the original on 20 May 2026. Retrieved 20 May 2026. 10. ↑ Quinn, Ben; Walker, Peter (15 May 2026). "Labour's NEC approves Burnham's byelection pathway back to parliament". The Guardian. ISSN "ISSN (identifier)") 0261-3077. Retrieved 17 May 2026. 11. ↑ "Andy Burnham certified as Labour candidate for Makerfield". BBC News. 19 May 2026. Archived from the original on 19 May 2026. Retrieved 19...

evidences.title: Who is standing in the Makerfield by-election?
evidences.content: The process ## What happens before polling day A by-election follows a set procedural timetable. Here is what must happen before votes are cast. 1 MP formally resigns The resignation of Josh Simons must be formally accepted by the Speaker. As of 15 May 2026 this is expected but still in process. 2 Writ is moved The House of Commons passes a writ ordering the by-election. This can happen as early as the next sitting day after the vacancy is confirmed. 3 Nomination period Candidates submit nomination papers and a £500 deposit. Voter registration and postal vote applications must be completed before deadlines set at this stage. 4 Polling day Voters in Makerfield cast ballots. The earliest date being discussed is 18 June 2026, but no polling day is confirmed yet. [...] ## What has changed, and why it matters The opening chapter of the race in chronological order — concise, time-stamped and easy to scan. Full timeline → 21 May 2026 ### Greens select Chris Kennedy Reported Wikipedia reports that the Green Party selected Chris Kennedy as its candidate on 21 May 2026. 20 May 2026 ### Notice of election confirms 18 June polling day Confirmed Wikipedia says Wigan Council published the not...

evidences.title: Makerfield By-Election – Volunteer on Polling Day | Labour – The Labour Party
evidences.content: What is the Makerfield by-election? The Makerfield by-election takes place on Thursday 18 June 2026. The constituency covers Wigan, Ashton-in-Makerfield and surrounding communities in Greater Manchester. Andy Burnham — the Mayor of Greater Manchester — is standing as the Labour candidate. Full voter information, including polling station locations, is available at wigan.gov.uk. People across Makerfield have the chance to send Westminster a clear message: these communities deserve to be heard. Too many people here feel politics isn’t working for them — and that’s exactly why this by-election matters. Andy Burnham is the candidate who will put Makerfield first. But winning takes all of us. [...] Members Area Go back Members FAQs Member login Resources Councillor hub Codes of conduct & complaints Conference Go back Annual Conference 2026 VolunteerDonateJoin Members Updates / Makerfield By-Election – Volunteer on Polling Day | Labour # Makerfield By-Election – Volunteer on Polling Day | Labour Jun 10, 2026 # Help win the Makerfield by-election – sign up for polling day now What is the Makerfield by-election?

evidences.title: Makerfield by-election: What we think has happened
evidences.content: with counting soon getting underway, we won't know for a good few hours, of course, the result of this Makerfield bi-election. Here is though a reminder of how recent election results there have gone or how they may or perhaps may not give us a clue of what to expect this time round. Well, in the 2024 general election, Labour held the seat, securing a majority of almost 5,400 over reform with a 45% vote share. Fast forward, though, to last month's local elections, and what a different story it was. In the eight council wards, which make up the majority of the constituency, reform received by far the most votes, almost 50% in fact. Labor was some way back on 26.9%. the Green Party in third on just over 10%. But according to four opinion polls over the last few weeks, the local election [...] opinion polls over the last few weeks, the local election results are not expected to be repeated. They've predicted on average that Labour candidate Andy Bernham will receive almost 46% of the vote. Reform further back on 40% their performance in the polls affected by Restore who the opinion polls suggest will get around 7.5% of the vote. Well, let's go back now to Harry at the count in Wiga...

evidences.title: Andy Burnham and Makerfield: Can a mayor be an MP?
evidences.content: Research Briefing Published Thursday, 21 May, 2026 Research Briefing Local government Mark Sandford Andy Burnham can stand to be elected as an MP while he is mayor of Greater Manchester, but if he wins he is immediately disqualified from the mayoralty. On 14 May 2026, Josh Simons, MP for Makerfield, resigned his seat in the House of Commons, triggering a by-election. Mr Simons stated that he had done this to give Andy Burnham, the mayor of Greater Manchester, an opportunity to stand for election to the House of Commons. The by-election will take place on Thursday 18 June 2026. Andy Burnham was confirmed as the Labour Party’s candidate on 19 May. Nominations for the by-election will close on 26 May 2026 (see the Library briefing By-election timetables). [...] Between the point at which the seat becomes vacant and a mayoral by-election, the statutory deputy mayor of Greater Manchester must become the acting mayor (see section 107C (5) of the Local Democracy, Economic Development and Construction Act 2009). The statutory deputy mayor is Paul Dennett. Mr Dennett is a member of the Greater Manchester combined authority and also directly elected mayor of Salford City Council. How does...

evidences.title: Metro Mayors – The Labour Party
evidences.content: ### Andy Burnham #### Mayor of Greater Manchester Andy Burnham was first elected as Mayor of Greater Manchester in May 2017 and re-elected in 2021, winning every ward in Greater Manchester. Responsible for shaping the future of Greater Manchester, Andy’s priorities include ending rough sleeping, transforming Greater Manchester into one of the greenest city regions in Europe, building a fully integrated public transport system and making Greater Manchester a great place to grow up, get on, and grow old. [...] Before being elected Mayor of Greater Manchester, Andy was MP for Leigh from 2001. In government, Andy held Ministerial positions at the Home Office, Department of Health and the Treasury. In 2008 he became Secretary of State for Culture, Media and Sport, before returning to Health as Secretary of State in 2009. In opposition, Andy served as Shadow Education Secretary, Shadow Health Secretary and Shadow Home Secretary. Andy lives in Leigh, Greater Manchester, with his family. ### Oliver Coppard #### Mayor of South Yorkshire Born and raised in the region, he took office as Mayor in May 2022, elected on a platform of restoring the pride, purpose and prosperity of South Yorkshi...

evidences.title: Andy Burnham – Britain's growing regional divides
evidences.content: Skip to content # Andy Burnham Andy Burnham has been the Mayor of Greater Manchester since 2017. Previously, he was Chief Secretary to the Treasury from 2007 to 2008, Culture Secretary from 2008 to 2009 and Health Secretary from 2009 to 2010. This interview was conducted on 28 April 2022. Q: Could you tell us about your role in growth and regional policy over the past few decades? [...] My role in Parliament was as a member of Parliament for what people would now call a Red Wall constituency, Leigh, a former mining area. I was the MP there for 16 years. Prior to that, I had been an adviser in the Labour government in the first term. In the middle part of the Labour government, I became a Minister – a junior minister at the Home Office and Health before becoming Chief Secretary to the Treasury, Culture Secretary, and then Health Secretary; and then in the opposition years a Shadow Cabinet member with Ed Balls and others. Eventually, I decided to come at regional policy from a different angle and put myself forward for the role of Mayor of Greater Manchester. In all of the ministerial jobs I had, I did often speak to the theme of regional inequality, it probably was the [...] to t...

evidences.title: The High-Stakes and Rather Unusual Makerfield By-Election
evidences.content: Having lost the two by-elections so far since the 2024 general election (in Runcorn and Helsby to Reform and in Gorton and Denton to the Greens) and bruised from the local and devolved elections in May, Labour faces an uphill task to hold the seat. The unusual circumstances in Makerfield make this one particularly difficult to predict, and the collision of local and national factors have created a uniquely high-stakes local skirmish. Mark Bennister. [...] In research analysing by-elections, Alia Middletonconcludedthat turnout is consistently lower in by-elections compared to general elections (though seats with lower turnouts in the earlier election see lower turnouts in by-elections too) and there is likely to be a swing against the government of the day. Of significance for Makerfield perhaps, Middleton’s researchnoted thatby-elections no longer routinely represent a way in which former MPs and heavyweights can be replanted into Westminster. ### By-election rules and regulations [...] Mark Bennister. _Mark Bennister is an experienced academic researching comparative political leadership, prime ministerial power, governance, public policy, and accountability in parliament. He i...

evidences.title: What is the state of play in the Makerfield by-election?
evidences.content: This time, the problem is compounded by the fact that, weeks before the 2024 election, Burnham won the mayoral election for Greater Manchester by a landslide. It’s certain that a fair number of them voted Labour for mayor but Reform for MP – and now think they supported Burnham all along. I reckon that Survation’s raw numbers are closer to the truth about what voters think they did than its weighted numbers. These are the voting intentions of Survation’s raw, unweighted sample, NOT adjusted for either turnout or recalled past vote: Andy Burnham (Labour) 47 per cent Robert Kenyon (Reform UK) 37 per cent None of the other candidates has more than six per cent. [...] 2. Which party they would support in a general election (Reform leads Labour by 45-34 per cent) 3. How likely they would be to vote in the coming by- election 4. Which party they would support in the by-election (no candidates names given; Labour and Reform both on 41 per cent). 5. Which candidate they would support in the by-election (names given; Burnham ahead by 43-40 per cent) [...] That is not all. Like all good pollsters, Survation adjusts its raw data to make sure its published figures reflect the population it...

evidences.content: Having lost the two by-elections so far since the 2024 general election (in Runcorn and Helsby to Reform and in Gorton and Denton to the Greens) and bruised from the local and devolved elections in May, Labour faces an uphill task to hold the seat. The unusual circumstances in Makerfield make this one particularly difficult to predict, and the collision of local and national factors have created a uniquely high-stakes local skirmish. Mark Bennister. [...] In research analysing by-elections, Alia Middleton concluded that turnout is consistently lower in by-elections compared to general elections (though seats with lower turnouts in the earlier election see lower turnouts in by-elections too) and there is likely to be a swing against the government of the day. Of significance for Makerfield perhaps, Middleton’s research noted that by-elections no longer routinely represent a way in which former MPs and heavyweights can be replanted into Westminster. ### By-election rules and regulations [...] There may be some parallels with Leyton in Makerfield, though in this case the Prime Minister will have mixed views about a potential Burnham return. If Burnham loses, it would be another blo...

evidences.title: Burham, Makerfield and Labour: the latest polls
evidences.content: 53% of Labour members believe that a new leader should take the party in a more left-wing direction. ### Makerfield by-election We have the first opinion poll, from Survation and published in The Sunday Times: Survation poll of Makerfield.png That sample size is a little on the low side, but constituency polling is tough, and it's large enough to give reasonable confidence that if there is something off with the poll, it isn't due to the sample being too small. Survation’s press release about the poll adds that: On a generic Westminster ballot, with no candidate names attached, Reform UK leads [...] Labour by eleven points in Makerfield. The eleven-point Reform lead in a generic ballot, set against a three-point Labour lead in the named-candidate by-election ballot, is the size of the Burnham effect in this poll: enough to put Labour narrowly in front of Reform, not enough to put the seat beyond reach. Meanwhile, Rob Ford once again has an excellent constituency profile: UPDATE: We have now had a handful of polls for the Makerfield by-election, so I’ve returned to the topic in Makerfield’s verdict: what the polls say before Britain’s most consequential by-election. ## Two great...

evidences.title: Majority vote explained
evidences.content: Joint Chiefs of Staff Quorum # Majority Vote A majority vote means that a proposition is selected by the majority of voters from a set of alternatives. In a majority vote, candidates with most votes in a constituency can enter Parliament. The voting process is suitable for electing direct representatives, which is why it is often used with direct elections. Relative and absolute majortiy With relative majority, a candidate has to obtain the most votes, or more votes than the other candidates in order to win the elections. With absolute majority, a candidate must obtain over a half of the votes cast, or 50% plus 1 votes. The majority vote is one of the two main types of election systems: the opposite of a majority vote is proportional representation. See also: #### Products [...] Relative and absolute majortiy With relative majority, a candidate has to obtain the most votes, or more votes than the other candidates in order to win the elections. With absolute majority, a candidate must obtain over a half of the votes cast, or 50% plus 1 votes. The majority vote is one of the two main types of election systems: the opposite of a majority vote is proportional representation. See als...

evidences.title: [PDF] 2/3 VOTE MAJORITY VOTE Robert's Rules of Order
evidences.content: The basic requirement for adoption of a motion by it's members, with a quorum, is a Majority Vote. A majority is more than half of the votes cast by persons legally entitled to vote, after taking blank votes and abstentions into account. Majority does not mean 51%. → → → → → → → → →Takes away membership or office. by 3 and then multiply by 2. Generally speaking, a 2/3 vote is required for adoption of any motion which: Suspends or modifies a rule of order already adopted. Prevents the introduction of a question to consideration. Closes, limits, or extends the limits of debate. [...] So of the 85 votes cast by members entitled to vote in the above example: (75 legal votes cast) - (4 blank votes cast) + (10 illegal votes cast) = 81 votes cast The Chair/President instructs the members (at a meeting with a quorum) to vote by writing "yes" or "no" on a piece of paper. Of the members present, 100 were entitled to vote, but 15 did not cast a ballot of any kind. Of the 85 votes cast: 75 were legal; 10 were illegal (the members wrote "maybe" MAJORITY VOTE The following is an example of Majority Vote: Example: In a situation with 1000 votes, the majority = 501 votes (not 51% which = 510 vo...

evidences.title: Elections in the United Kingdom - Wikipedia
evidences.content: Elections are administered locally: in each lower-tier local authority, the polling procedure is operated by the returning officer or acting returning officer, and the compiling and maintenance of the electoral roll by the electoral registration officer (except in Northern Ireland, where the Electoral Office for Northern Ireland assumes both responsibilities). The Electoral Commission "Electoral Commission (United Kingdom)") sets standards for and issues guidelines to returning officers and electoral registration officers, and is responsible for nationwide electoral administration (such as the registration of political parties and directing the administration of national referendums). ## Electoral registration [edit] [...] The returning officer must issue and send out postal ballot packs 'as soon as is practicable' (i.e. as soon as possible after the close of nominations at 4pm 19 working days before polling day). Where an elector has applied for a postal ballot to be sent to an overseas address, the returning officer should prioritise the dispatch of their postal ballot packs (over those sent to UK addresses), send them by air mail and ensure that the postal ballot pack include...

evidences.title: Explained: how by-elections happen - by Eliot Wilson
evidences.content: The process is, again, less complicated than it may first appear. Once the seat in question is vacant, a motion is made to direct the Speaker to issue his warrant to the Clerk of the Crown in Chancery. Any Member can make the motion but by convention the task is carried out by a whip, usually the Chief Whip, from the party which previously held the seat; it will therefore probably be the Government Chief Whip, Jonathan Reynolds, who makes the motion in respect of the Makerfield by-election. The moving of the writ is technically a matter of privilege, so it may be taken without notice and can take precedence over any other business. On Monday, for example, warrants were issued for writs for by-elections in Aberdeen South and Arbroath and Broughty Ferry, Stephen Flynn and Stephen Gethins of [...] Onwards to Makerfield… The position of Clerk of the Crown in Chancery was held jointly with that of Permanent Secretary to the Lord Chancellor’s Department 1885-2003 and Permanent Secretary to the Department for Constitutional Affairs 2003-07. For by-elections in constituencies in Northern Ireland, the warrant is issued to the Clerk of the Crown for Northern Ireland (currently Andrew Well...

evidences.title: What does Makerfield make of by-election and can Burnham win?
evidences.content: At the general election in 2024, Simons won the seat with 45.2% of the vote, a majority of 5,399. Reform came in second with 31.8% of the vote, with the Conservatives in third, Lib Dems in fourth and Greens in fifth. The constituency boundaries were changed slightly in that election, but the seat has been Labour-held for decades. This seat is 29th on Reform's target list and the party needs a swing of just over 6.7% to win it. There are no precise figures for how the parties performed in Makerfield during the local elections because the ward boundaries do not fully align with the constituency boundaries. But roughly, Reform pulled in 50% of the vote share, with Labour at 27%, the Greens at 10%, Conservatives at 7% and Lib Dems at 4%. [...] Burnham has been given permission by officers on Labour's National Executive Committee to stand in the candidate selection process in the Makerfield by-election. He will now have to be approved by the local Labour Party in Makerfield. Burnham was re-elected for a second term as Greater Manchester mayor in May 2021 and a third in May 2024, with 63.4% of the vote - in Wigan, he received 66% of the vote. Polling expert Sir John Curtice told Polit...

evidences.content: Andy for Makerfield - Andy for Makerfield # Andy Burnham for Makerfield – Official By-Election Campaign Site ## Join me on polling day – let’s win in Makerfield! On Thursday 18 June, people across Makerfield have the chance to send Westminster a clear message: our communities deserve to be heard. Too many people here feel politics is not working for them, and they are right. That is why this campaign matters. These proud working-class communities represent the very best of our country, and polling day is our opportunity to stand together and fight for the better future they deserve. Every conversation, every door knocked, every voter reminded can make the difference. Join us on polling day, be part of the Labour team in Makerfield, and help us win. Join me on polling day [...] Join me on polling day ## What's New In The Campaign All Hindley and Hindley Green Platt Bridge Winstanley Ashton and Bryn ## Platt Bridge: No Family Should Live In Fear Of Flooding Platt Bridge 01 June 2026 ## Bringing Healthcare Back To Orrell and Billinge Orrell and Billinge 27 May 2026 ## Platt Bridge: A Community That Looks After Each Other Platt Bridge 27 May 2026

evidences.title: Key roles and planning | College of Policing
evidences.content: ### Returning officer The returning officer is responsible for ensuring that elections are conducted in accordance with the law. They are personally liable for the conduct of an election. There are a number of key statutory responsibilities to ensure the effective conduct of any election that the returning officer must comply with (for example, the arrangements for polling day and the counting of votes). The returning officer is also responsible for the management of local elections. For local council referendums, council tax referendums or mayoral referendums, the returning officer becomes the counting officer. The acting returning officer has this responsibility for a UK parliamentary election. [...] The Commission provides guidance to returning officers on delivering well-run elections and monitors how well they perform against performance standards. Each year the Commission publishes reports on how well elections were run and makes recommendations to improve future elections. The Commission also publishes reports following referendums. [...] Police attendance at the briefing for candidates and agents held by the returning officer demonstrates the joint approach being taken b...
